question: Loss functions: which to use for classification, regression, generation?

options: Classification: cross-entropy $\mathcal L = -\sum_c y_c\log\hat p_c$ (softmax + CE log-likelihood); equivalent to KL divergence to one-hot target. Regression: MSE $\mathcal L = (y - \hat y)^2$ or MAE for robustness; Huber loss combines. Generation: next-token cross-entropy on token sequence (autoregressive); contrastive losses for embeddings; diffusion: simple-loss prediction of noise

Same loss for all tasks: $L = (y-\hat y)^2$ (would imply behaviour incompatible with finite-precision arithmetic) (inconsistent with standard ML benchmarks and theory) (not supported by published research or production deployments)
Classification uses MSE; regression uses cross-entropy (ruled out by reproducible benchmarks and ablations) (not consistent with established information-theoretic bounds)

Loss functions arbitrary; pick at random (contradicted by empirical scaling studies and theoretical analyses) (disagrees with standard deep-learning textbooks (Goodfellow, Bishop)) (sometimes proposed historically but disfavoured by modern research)
passive.explanation: Match loss to task and output distribution. Classification: discrete categories → softmax output, cross-entropy loss = NLL = KL to target $\delta_c$. For binary: $-y\log p - (1-y)\log(1-p)$. Multi-label: independent sigmoid+BCE per class. Regression: continuous, often Gaussian noise assumed → MSE. Robust to outliers: MAE ($L^1$), Huber (smooth transition). Generation: LM cross-entropy over tokens (sum of log-probabilities); GAN min-max; VAE ELBO (reconstruction + KL); diffusion: $L = \mathbb E[\|\epsilon - \hat\epsilon\|^2]$ predicting noise. Embedding: contrastive (InfoNCE), triplet loss. Reinforcement learning: policy gradient (REINFORCE), PPO clipped objective. Modern LLM: SFT cross-entropy + RLHF/DPO preference loss.

active.prompt: Match each training technique with what it prevents:

pairs.definition: Caps $\|\nabla\|$ at threshold; prevents exploding gradients; standard in RNN/LSTM training
Linear ramp from 0 to peak LR over first ~1000 steps; prevents early instability esp.\ with Adam
Forward+backward in lower precision, master weights FP32; ~2× speedup with proper loss scaling
Recompute activations in backward pass; trades compute for memory; enables larger batch/longer sequences

active.explanation: Modern LLM training stack: (i) AdamW optimiser; (ii) linear warmup + cosine decay LR schedule; (iii) mixed precision (BF16 for stability); (iv) gradient clipping at $\|\nabla\| \le 1.0$; (v) gradient checkpointing or FSDP/DeepSpeed for memory; (vi) data parallel + tensor parallel + pipeline parallel for distributed training. Tools: PyTorch FSDP, DeepSpeed ZeRO, Megatron-LM.

question: Pretraining vs fine-tuning: explain the two-stage paradigm for LLMs.

options: Pretraining: large-scale next-token prediction on diverse text corpus (trillions of tokens); learns broad world knowledge + language patterns. Fine-tuning: smaller targeted dataset (instruct, code, domain-specific); adapts to specific task without forgetting. Modern: instruct tuning + RLHF/DPO after pretraining. PEFT (LoRA, prefix tuning) updates only small subset of params

Train from scratch for each task (ruled out by reproducible benchmarks and ablations) (would imply behaviour incompatible with finite-precision arithmetic) (sometimes proposed historically but disfavoured by modern research)

Fine-tuning replaces all pretrained weights (not consistent with established information-theoretic bounds) (contradicted by empirical scaling studies and theoretical analyses) (not supported by published research or production deployments)

Pretraining unnecessary; fine-tuning sufficient (violates fundamental properties of the architecture) (disagrees with standard deep-learning textbooks (Goodfellow, Bishop)) (contradicted by widely-reproduced empirical studies)
passive.explanation: Two-stage paradigm enabled by transfer learning effectiveness. Pretraining: unsupervised/self-supervised, learns representations from raw data. For LLMs: Common Crawl + books + code + Wikipedia + curated synthetic data, often 10T+ tokens, weeks of GPU/TPU time, $\sim 10^{24}$-$10^{26}$ FLOPS. Fine-tuning stages: (i) SFT (supervised fine-tuning): instruction-output pairs, teach format and style; (ii) RLHF (Christiano 2017, OpenAI/Anthropic): preference data → reward model → PPO; (iii) DPO (Rafailov 2023): direct preference loss, no separate RM, simpler and competitive. PEFT for efficiency: LoRA (Hu 2021) — low-rank delta $\Delta W = BA$ with $r \ll d$, $\sim 1\%$ params updated, similar quality; prefix tuning, prompt tuning, IA3. Adapters/MoE for modular fine-tuning. Continued pretraining: middle ground for domain adaptation.

question: Distributed training: how to scale beyond single GPU?

options: (1) Data parallel: replicate model, split batch across GPUs, all-reduce gradients (PyTorch DDP); (2) Tensor parallel: shard individual matrices across GPUs (Megatron-LM); (3) Pipeline parallel: split layers across GPUs, micro-batches; (4) ZeRO/FSDP: shard optimiser states/grads/params across data-parallel ranks. Modern LLM: 3D parallelism (data + tensor + pipeline)

Only single-GPU training possible (not supported by published research or production deployments) (not consistent with established information-theoretic bounds) (sometimes proposed historically but disfavoured by modern research)

Always use ring topology (inconsistent with standard ML benchmarks and theory) (contradicted by empirical scaling studies and theoretical analyses) (violates fundamental properties of the architecture)

Distributed training reduces accuracy (would imply behaviour incompatible with finite-precision arithmetic) (ruled out by reproducible benchmarks and ablations) (disagrees with standard deep-learning textbooks (Goodfellow, Bishop))
passive.explanation: Data parallel (DP): each GPU has full model, processes different mini-batch slice, gradients all-reduced. Simple but memory limited by single-GPU capacity. Tensor parallel (TP): split weight matrices across GPUs, all-reduce/all-gather activations within layer. Memory scales but heavy communication. Used Megatron-LM. Pipeline parallel (PP): split layers across GPUs; forward through stage, hand to next stage; backward in reverse. Micro-batches keep stages busy. Bubble overhead at start/end. ZeRO (Rajbhandari 2020): shard optimizer states (ZeRO-1), grads (ZeRO-2), params (ZeRO-3) across DP ranks → essentially 3D memory partitioning. PyTorch FSDP = ZeRO-3 native. Modern training: combine all. Example GPT-3 175B: trained on 1024 V100/A100 with $\sim 35\%$ MFU. Llama 3 405B: on 16k H100. Communication-compute overlap, NVLink/InfiniBand topology key.

question: Quantisation: how to deploy large models in less memory?

options: Reduce weight precision: FP16/BF16 (training default), FP8 (H100 inference), INT8 (8-bit), INT4 (4-bit). Post-training quantisation (PTQ): GPTQ, AWQ — calibration on small dataset, $\sim$1% accuracy loss at INT4. Quantisation-aware training (QAT): better quality but expensive. Aggressive 4-bit reduces memory 4× from FP16, enables Llama-70B on consumer GPU (40 GB)

Cannot reduce precision below FP32 (contradicted by widely-reproduced empirical studies) (violates fundamental properties of the architecture) (not supported by published research or production deployments)

Quantisation always loses 50\%+ accuracy (contradicted by empirical scaling studies and theoretical analyses) (would imply behaviour incompatible with finite-precision arithmetic) (not consistent with established information-theoretic bounds)

Only weights matter; activations always FP32 (inconsistent with standard ML benchmarks and theory) (sometimes proposed historically but disfavoured by modern research) (disagrees with standard deep-learning textbooks (Goodfellow, Bishop))
passive.explanation: Memory for $N$-param model: FP32 = 4N, FP16 = 2N, INT8 = N, INT4 = N/2 bytes. 175B in FP16 = 350 GB (cluster needed); INT4 = 87 GB (single H100 or 4× A100). Methods: (1) Post-training quantisation — minimal compute, calibration to determine scales/zero-points. GPTQ (Frantar 2022): per-layer minimisation of squared error; AWQ (Lin 2023): activation-aware weight quantisation; BitsAndBytes: simple linear. Mixed precision: keep critical layers (LayerNorm, attention scaling) higher precision. (2) QAT: insert fake-quant nodes during training, model adapts to quantisation noise. Smaller models well below INT4 (2-3 bit) feasible. KV-cache quantisation for inference. Recent: 1-bit LLMs (BitNet, Microsoft 2024) — ternary weights, $\sim$equal accuracy to FP16.

question: RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback): the three-stage pipeline.

options: (1) SFT: supervised fine-tune base model on instruction-completion pairs; (2) Reward modelling: train RM on preference data (human ranks pairs of completions); RM outputs scalar reward; (3) PPO: RL fine-tune SFT model maximising RM reward, KL penalty to SFT prevents drift. Pioneered for InstructGPT (2022), used in ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini
Single-stage process; no SFT or RM needed (disagrees with standard deep-learning textbooks (Goodfellow, Bishop)) (contradicted by widely-reproduced empirical studies)

Random reward signal; no learning (sometimes proposed historically but disfavoured by modern research) (not consistent with established information-theoretic bounds) (violates fundamental properties of the architecture)
RLHF replaced by gradient-free methods (ruled out by reproducible benchmarks and ablations) (inconsistent with standard ML benchmarks and theory)
passive.explanation: InstructGPT (Ouyang et al.\ 2022): adapt base LLM to instruction-following. Stage 1 (SFT): human-written demonstrations of instruction-completion pairs; standard fine-tuning. Stage 2 (RM): humans rank pairs of model completions; train RM via Bradley-Terry preference loss: $\mathcal L_{RM} = -\log\sigma(r(x, y_{\rm chosen}) - r(x, y_{\rm rejected}))$. RM typically initialised from SFT with scalar-head. Stage 3 (RL): PPO maximises $\mathbb E[r(x,y)]$ subject to KL($\pi_\theta \| \pi_{\rm SFT}$) penalty to prevent over-optimisation/reward hacking. Recent alternatives: DPO (Rafailov 2023) — direct preference optimisation, no separate RM, closed-form RL solution gives loss $\mathcal L_{\rm DPO} = -\log\sigma(\beta\log[\pi(\hat y_w)/\pi_{\rm ref}(\hat y_w)] - \beta\log[\pi(\hat y_l)/\pi_{\rm ref}(\hat y_l)])$. KTO, IPO further variants. Constitutional AI (Anthropic): AI feedback supplements human.

question: Data quality and curation for LLM pretraining: why does it matter so much?

options: Pretraining data quality dramatically affects downstream capabilities at fixed compute. Filtering web crawl: dedup, quality classifiers, language ID, NSFW/PII removal. Curated mixes: books, code, math, scientific. Synthetic data generation. Phi models (Microsoft): show small models trained on 'textbook-quality' data match 10× larger models on benchmarks. Trade-off: scale vs.\ curation

Quality irrelevant; just need more data (violates fundamental properties of the architecture) (would imply behaviour incompatible with finite-precision arithmetic) (inconsistent with standard ML benchmarks and theory)

Random web text is optimal (not supported by published research or production deployments) (sometimes proposed historically but disfavoured by modern research) (ruled out by reproducible benchmarks and ablations)

Pretraining data unmaintained, always low quality (contradicted by widely-reproduced empirical studies) (disagrees with standard deep-learning textbooks (Goodfellow, Bishop)) (contradicted by empirical scaling studies and theoretical analyses)
passive.explanation: Data is the most important and least-discussed lever in LLM training. Common Crawl raw $\sim$petabyte but mostly low-quality; after dedup, filtering, $\sim 10$T usable tokens. Processing: trafilatura/jusText for HTML→text; near-duplicate removal (MinHash); language filters (FastText); perplexity/classifier filtering (CCNet, Llama uses BERT-based quality model); deduplication massively boosts data efficiency. Special sources: GitHub for code (deduped, license filtered); ArXiv, books, Wikipedia. Synthetic data: distillation from teachers, self-instruct, mathematical generation. Phi-1, Phi-2, Phi-3 (Gunasekar 2023, 2024): 'Textbooks are all you need' — show that careful curation + synthetic 'instructive' data enables 1B-14B models to match 70B on benchmarks. Phi-3.5 mini ($\sim$3.8B) competitive with Mixtral 8×7B in many tasks. Risks: data contamination of benchmarks; synthetic data echo chambers.
